narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam, tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as schools prioritize admitting students predicted to succeed right away. This decision hinged on a few key aspects of your academic profile, with your undergraduate GPA, Year 3 school decile ranking, and family income bracket standing out as the strongest influences. Your above-average undergrad GPA worked strongly in your favor, pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass by demonstrating a level of college-level academic strength that the model associates with bar success. On the flip side, your Year 3 decile ranking of 2—well below the average of about 5.5—had the most significant pull in the opposite direction, signaling to the model that your performance relative to peers in that later year raised concerns about sustaining momentum through law school and the bar. Your lower-middle family income bracket, compared to higher averages in the dataset, provided a moderate positive nudge toward passing, as the model links such backgrounds to resilience and motivation in some cases. Overall, these factors placed you in a riskier category than many applicants, where strong early college grades weren't enough to offset the dip in your later school deciles, even with your full-time student status aligning with the vast majority at 92.7%. Your LSAT score of 28, notably below the 36.7 average, played a supporting role but wasn't among the top drivers here. While this outcome is disappointing, especially given your respectable undergrad record, it reflects how the model weighs sustained performance across years more heavily for bar predictions. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, seeking ways to improve your decile rankings through targeted academic support, and gathering letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience from a lower-middle income background. Strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor for other programs. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, so you're far from out of options.
